critique.summary: The article announces the release of Kitten TTS v0.8, an open-source, lightweight text-to-speech library with models ranging from 15M to 80M parameters and sizes between 25 MB and 80 MB. It highlights the library's CPU-optimized performance, multiple built-in voices, and features such as adjustable speech speed and text preprocessing.
mainPoints: Kitten TTS v0.8 offers three model sizes: 15M (nano), 40M (micro), and 80M (mini) parameters.
Models range in size from 25 MB (int8 quantized nano model) to 80 MB, suitable for edge deployment without requiring a GPU.
The library supports 8 built-in voices and outputs high-quality 24 kHz audio.
It is CPU-optimized using ONNX for efficient inference.
The project is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and provides commercial support options.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Kitten TTS are consistent with publicly available information on the official GitHub repository and related documentation. The model sizes, parameter counts, and features such as CPU optimization and multiple voices are accurately described. The project is indeed open-source and uses ONNX for inference. The mention of commercial support and the license type also align with the repository details. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: User experience and audio quality comparisons with other TTS models are not discussed.
Potential limitations or issues, such as those reported with the int8 quantized nano model, are briefly mentioned but not elaborated.
The article does not provide independent benchmarks or third-party reviews of the model's performance.
Compatibility with various operating systems and integration complexity for developers is only briefly mentioned.
The roadmap and future development plans could impact the stability and features of the library.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the new Kitten TTS models, highlighting their small size and impressive performance, but also noting issues with dependencies, voice quality, and pronunciation. Users express interest in features like expressive control, multilingual support, and offline usability, while some raise concerns about installation complexity and model transparency.

comments.author: dawdler-purge
comments.text:
I created a CLI wrapper for Kitten TTS: https://github.com/newptcai/purr

BTW, it seems that kitten (the Python package) has the following chain of dependencies: kittentts → misaki[en] → spacy-curated-transformers

So if you install it directly via uv, it will pull torch and NVIDIA CUDA packages (several GB), which are not needed to run kitten.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate insight into the dependency issues that can affect installation size and complexity, which is a common concern in ML projects.

comments.author: kevin42
comments.text:
What I love about OpenClaw is that I was able to send it a message on Discord with just this github URL and it started sending me voice messages using it within a few minutes. It also gave me a bunch of different benchmarks and sample audio.

I'm impressed with the quality given the size. I don't love the voices, but it's not bad. Running on an intel 9700 CPU, it's about 1.5x realtime using the 80M model. It wasn't any faster running on a 3080 GPU though.
comments.analysis: The comment offers practical user experience with the model's performance and quality, providing useful benchmarks and observations.

comments.author: __fst__
comments.text:
Was playing around a bit and for its size it's very impressive. Just has issues pronounciating numbers. I tried to let it generate "Startup finished in 135 ms."

I didn't expect it to pronounciate 'ms' correctly, but the number sounded just like noise. Eventually I got an acceptable result for the string "Startup finished in one hundred and thirty five seconds.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a specific limitation in the model's pronunciation capabilities, which is valuable feedback for developers and users.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text: Good on device TTS is an amazing accessibility tool. Thank you for building this. Way too many of devices that use it rely on online services, this is much preferred.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly emphasizes the importance of offline TTS for accessibility and privacy, aligning with broader user needs.

comments.author: armcat
comments.text: This is awesome, well done. Been doing lot of work with voice assistants, if you can replicate voice cloning Qwen3-TTS into this small factor, you will be absolute legends!
comments.analysis: This comment suggests a valuable future direction for the project, showing understanding of the field and user desires.

comments.author: ks2048
comments.text:
There's a number of recent, good quality, small TTS models.

If the author doesn't describe some detail about the data, training, or a novel architecture, etc, I only assume they just took another one, do a little finetuning, and repackage as a new product.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about transparency and novelty in ML model releases, encouraging more detailed disclosures.

comments.author: Remi_Etien
comments.text: 25MB is impressive. What's the tradeoff vs the 80M model — is it mainly voice quality or does it also affect pronunciation accuracy on less common words?
comments.analysis: This comment asks relevant technical questions about model size versus quality tradeoffs, indicating an informed perspective.

comments.author: PunchyHamster
comments.text: I ran install instructions and it took 7.1GB of deps, tf you mean "tiny" ?
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration about the large dependency footprint despite the small model size, a common pain point for users.

comments.author: tredre3
comments.text:
This is something I've been looking for (the <50MB models in particular). Unfortunately my feedback is as follows:

      Downloading https://github.com/KittenML/KittenTTS/releases/download/0.8.1/kittentts-0.8.1-py3-none-any.whl (22 kB)
    Collecting num2words (from kittentts==0.8.1)
      Using cached num2words-0.5.14-py3-none-any.whl.metadata (13 kB)
    Collecting spacy (from kittentts==0.8.1)
      Using cached spacy-3.8.11-cp314-cp314-win_amd64.whl.metadata (28 kB)
    Collecting espeakng_loader (from kittentts==0.8.1)
      Using cached espeakng_loader-0.2.4-py3-none-win_amd64.whl.metadata (1.3 kB)
    INFO: pip is looking at multiple versions of kittentts to determine which version is compatible with other requirements. This could take a while.
    ERROR: Ignored the following versions that require a different python version: 0.7.10 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.11 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.12 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.13 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.14 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.15 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.16 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.17 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.5 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.6 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.7 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.8 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.7.9 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.8.0 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.8.1 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.8.2 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.8.3 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.8.4 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.9.0 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.9.2 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.9.3 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 0.9.4 Requires-Python >=3.8,<3.13; 3.8.3 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.13; 3.8.5 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.13; 3.8.6 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.13; 3.8.7 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.14; 3.8.8 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.14; 3.8.9 Requires-Python >=3.9,<3.14
    ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement misaki>=0.9.4 (from kittentts) (from versions: 0.1.0, 0.3.0, 0.3.5, 0.3.9, 0.4.0, 0.4.4, 0.4.5, 0.4.6, 0.4.7, 0.4.8, 0.4.9, 0.5.0, 0.5.1, 0.5.2, 0.5.3, 0.5.4, 0.5.5, 0.5.6, 0.5.7, 0.5.8, 0.5.9, 0.6.0, 0.6.1, 0.6.2, 0.6.3, 0.6.4, 0.6.5, 0.6.6, 0.6.7, 0.7.0, 0.7.1, 0.7.2, 0.7.3, 0.7.4)
    ERROR: No matching distribution found for misaki>=0.9.4
I realize that I can run a multiple versions of python on my system, and use venv to managed them (or whatever equivalent is now trendy), but as I near retirement age all those deep dependencies nets required by modern software is really depressing me. Have you ever tried to build a node app that hasn't been updated in 18 months? It can't be done. Old man yelling at cloud I guess shrugs.
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed account of installation challenges, reflecting real-world difficulties in managing modern Python ML environments.

comments.author: _hzw
comments.text: I'd love to see a monolingual Japanese model sometime in the future. Qwen3-tts works for Japanese in general, but from time to time it will mix with some Mandarin in between, making it unusable.
comments.analysis: Highlights a specific language limitation relevant to non-English users, suggesting an important area for improvement.
